title: The Scenery of the Park, Then and Now
description: A date at the park after school—then, years later, a date at the same park after work. A story of growing up, staying together, and the quiet beauty of choosing each other every day.
content:
After school, Adachi told me, 'Let's go on a date!' Of course, I said 'Sure!'—right away. Well… to be precise, she actually asked in her usual, timid way: 'C-can we go on a date?' which honestly made me think: Adachi really is adorable, as always. And yes, I did answer with that exact 'Sure!'

Her lowered face relaxed a little when she heard my reply. Good—that's better.

'So, where should we go?'

There aren't many places to go for an after-school date in this small town. The mall we always go to… my house… We've never gone to karaoke together—maybe we can try that next time. I'm not really in that mood today, but it might be fun someday.

'Do you have anywhere you want to go, Adachi?'

'As long as it's somewhere you want to go, I'm fine with anything.'

'Uh… I see.'

She gave me the hardest possible answer. Walking down the hallway toward the shoe lockers, even the dryness stinging my nose felt like a sign that the weather was turning hot. We were in that awkward spring-to-summer season. The uniforms had switched to summer ones, and Adachi's bare upper arms in her short-sleeved shirt were dazzling. I was happily admiring them when she suddenly noticed and met my gaze.

She smiled—awkwardly, of course. Adachi only ever smiles at me, yet she can't seem to smile properly in front of me. I always wonder what I should do about that, but… maybe that clumsy part is exactly what makes her 'Adachi.'

'Where should we go…'

'Anywhere is fine…'

She repeated herself like she was chewing the words. As long as she can be alone with me, she's satisfied. Realizing that made me feel a little embarrassed.

'Oh—'

Going down the stairs, I suddenly remembered something worrying. I opened my bag and checked my wallet.

'…Yep.'

'Shimamura?'

'My wallet's empty for the month.' I showed her. No big bills, barely any coins remaining.

'We went on too many dates this month.'

It's because she kept inviting me so sweetly and clingingly that I always said yes. And I had fun, sure… but there's something she and I differ on.

Money.

Adachi works part-time whenever she has free time. Honestly, she's amazing.

'Um… sorry.'

She must've realized I spent a lot, so she apologized. But she really didn't have to.

I spent the money because I wanted to.

'Well, isn't it nice? Using my money to spend time with you.'

There's really no better use for it.

'I see— pffft… heh, heh, heh…'

Her face darkened for a moment, then she puffed her cheeks and let out a weird noise. Judging from experience… she's laughing, so she must be happy.

'U-um, then… c-can we go to your house?'

'Yeah, that's fine… or we could go to the park?'

It was the perfect season to be outside. And really, all Adachi wanted was time with me. A park date might sound boring, but it works.

'O-okay…'

And so, we decided on the park.

We left school and walked quite a distance before I hopped onto the back of her bike. She always carries me like this, but two people on one bike definitely makes the pedals heavy. I felt a little bad for letting her do all the work.

'Let's buy drinks.'

I pointed to the convenience store on the way. We parked the bike.

As we approached the entrance, Adachi gave me a shy smile.

'Is your wallet okay?'

She really meant 'Do you want me to pay?' I puffed myself up and went, 'Heh heh heh,' pretending everything was fine.

She bought her usual bottled water. I bought a cheap soft-pack orange juice. Bottled drinks were too expensive for my current financial crisis—maybe next time, when I'm rich again.

We continued toward the park I remembered. I asked if Adachi ever went there as a kid. 'Probably not,' she said. Knowing what her mom is like, that made sense. Not everyone's mom takes them to parks like mine did… Though honestly, too many moms like mine might be a problem—every slide would be occupied forever.

Following scattered memories, we reached the small, tree-filled park near a shrine. I used to come here with my closest friend back then.

There was nobody else around. Maybe kids didn't play at parks anymore. It was a shame—the swings were still nice. I'd feel bad if they ever got removed.

The bench looked clean, like someone actually maintained it. No sticky residue, no trash. We brushed off fallen leaves and sat together.

Sunlight filtered between the branches and danced across us.

'I feel like I'm going to get more melanin.'

'Huh…?'

Still confused, Adachi opened her water. Meanwhile, I struggled to poke the straw into my juice. Seeing my battle, Adachi held out her hand. I handed it to her, and—naturally, like the world favored her—the straw went in immediately.

'Here.'

'Thanks.'

I accepted it and patted her head. She complained, 'Don't treat me like a kid…' but she let me pet her anyway.

'Want me to treat you like a mother instead?'

'No thank you…'

'Ah, puberty.' That's not the point, probably.

I sipped my orange juice while following the wind with my gaze. Branches swayed above, carrying the soft smell of trees. It wasn't floral—just gentle.

'How much longer can we spend time like this?'

'Huh?'

'Exams are coming. We probably won't be able to hang out like this for much longer.'

My grades, my future school… everything was still undecided.

'We won't be high schoolers anymore.'

I pointed to her uniform. Soon, I wouldn't get to see Adachi wearing it. The thought made me stare at her. She shrank a little, embarrassed—adorably so. Then, still hunched, she took my hand.

'But… we'll still be together, right?'

'Of course.'

We just wouldn't be in the same classroom anymore. That alone could change things—we had no way of knowing how. Relationships shift with time, with distance, with the smallest change in routine.

I'd let someone slip away once. Let the distance settle between us.

Maybe that's why… Now that I want this to last, I can't figure out how to say what I feel. But Adachi said: 'No matter how old I get, I want to sit in parks like this with you… every day, if possible.'

'...Mm—'

It was so sudden my mouth hesitated.

'That's basically… you know.'

I sipped my juice, eyeing her.

'You know?'

'A proposal.'

'Ah…'

She nodded vaguely. Surprisingly, she wasn't flustered. I honestly expected her to panic.

She took a slow sip of water and gave me a shy smile 'Because I've already decided—I want to be with you for my entire life.'

'…Haha. So it was a proposal.'

Never thought I'd get proposed to at this age. Not many high schoolers think that far ahead.

'For your entire life, huh?'

'Is that… bad?'

Her eyes wavered anxiously. I wanted to tease her, but if I made her cry, I'd feel guilty.

'It's not bad. Not bad at all. Actually, it's great. But—let me ask.'

I padded it with lots of softeners. Because my question wasn't my real answer.

'What if I said no?'

I lowered my gaze, repeating the question inside my head.

Then—'I would keep trying. Until you say yes.'

She didn't hesitate. Sunlight filtered through the leaves like warm water.

'…………………'

I leaned back, shielding my face.

'Shimamura?'

'You're… shining.'

'Huh?'

'Your purity. It's too much. I'm being purified.'

Positive, beautiful, simple. Her answer was the opposite of what I expected, and that embarrassed me.

I thought she'd cry. I imagined her panicking, clinging to me.

But maybe she's stronger than I thought. Her determination—her absolute refusal to let me go—is real. She likes me that much.

And every time I feel that affection, I think: Why? Why am I loved this much?

I don't see myself the way she sees me… But I wish I could.

Maybe it's that gap in our sense of love that makes Adachi so frantic sometimes.

I'm not at her level… but still—If Adachi ever left me because of some misunderstanding, I'd chase after her. If she didn't come see me for three days, I'd go find her.

'…Hah.'

I don't show these things. I rarely let anything surface. But maybe I'm not so different from her after all.

Because the truth is simple: I can't imagine a life where she isn't beside me.

'There's still a part of me you haven't purified.'

'What do you mean…?'

'You're amazing, Adachi. You seem so soft, but you're unyielding.'

'S-soft…?'

'Well, because you're soft, you bounce back.'

'S-soft—what does that even—'

'Want some orange juice?'

I forced the subject change and handed her the juice. Adachi took it softly—very softly—and stared at the straw, frozen. Her cheeks turned plum-colored as she hesitated.

Her bizarre balance between boldness and shyness made me laugh.

'Want me to wipe the straw?'

'N-no need!'

She shook her head like her life depended on it. She sipped the juice, face bright red. Probably couldn't even taste it.

She handed the juice back and looked at the water bottle in her hand.

'You want water?'

She offered it to me.

'Then I won't hold back.'

I gulped it down. Cold, clear water flowed through me—refreshing in a way other drinks can't be.

'I kind of wish I bought juice too.'

'This is fine. Water tastes like you.'

I handed it back. She stared at the label, confused.

Her blush returned.

What was she imagining?

'Okay, half of that was a lie.'

'What?'

The old Adachi like was water—colorless, transparent, dreamy. Being with me turned her into something richer, more vivid. Sometimes I feel nostalgic about that, but time only moves forward.

Thinking like that made me strangely optimistic.

'…Mm.'

And I'd been proposed to, so… Maybe I should study hard once I get home today.

Surely, we can reach the future we imagine. At least for now, the world feels bright.

※

On the way home, I spotted Adachi's silhouette ahead of me. I spent about ten seconds agonizing over how to approach her. Should I just walk up normally? I could, but that felt like such a waste. In moments like this, I guess I really am a bit like my mother.

Still, seeing Adachi walk with her back so straight even after work—honestly, it impressed me. She used to slouch a little. Her dark hair blended into the deepening dusk, like the sky folding into night. Beautiful as it was, this wasn't the time to admire her.

After all, if I delayed any further, she'd reach home before I called out. So I tried, 'Hey, Adachi—' I started with a small voice, curious how quietly I could speak before she noticed.

And then, for a moment, I could've sworn I saw her ears twitch like a startled puppy. Even though my voice was soft, she reacted immediately and turned around.

Adachi smiled the moment she saw me and walked over. Seeing her approach head-on… she really had an amazing figure. Absolutely flawless. I wondered what she ate to keep such a body—but then realized we eat basically the same things.

'…Well, I guess that's just how it is sometimes.'

We walked toward each other, closing the distance quickly.

'It's rare for us both to be home this early,' she said.

'Yeah.'

Usually once we part ways in the morning, we hardly see each other again until night fully settles. I tilted my head up to the sky—sunset slanting into the dusk. Wait, no. It was the sunset being cut through by the approaching night.

Looking at that dividing line between day and night always stirs a strange restlessness in me—but somehow it's an exciting restlessness.

'How about we take the long way home?' I suggested.

'The long way?'

'There's a park over there. Let's call it a date.'

Adachi's expression softened instantly.

'That's nostalgic,' she murmured.

'Back when I didn't have money, we'd just hang out at each other's places or go to the park,' I said.

Even now, even with a bit of money, we still go to that park behind our apartment.

'If we get hungry, we can head back.'

'N-no, let's… let's go on a d-date.'

She stumbled over the word a little—classic Adachi.

'Wanna buy drinks?'

'Ah, I already did.'

I lifted the bottle of orange juice I'd been holding and shook it a little.

'Shimamura?'

'Showing off that I'm rich now.'

Adachi tilted her head with a confused smile. Since growing up, her expressions had become calmer—very charming. We reached the convenience store we'd passed earlier, and I waited outside while she went in. Someone once said this habit shows personality. If I'm not buying anything, I wait at the entrance. Adachi, on the other hand, always comes inside with me—even if she's not buying.

Neither is good or bad—it's just who we are.

Under the store's bright lights, watching car headlights pass by gave me that perfect sense that the day was ending. A light, pleasant fatigue. And knowing tomorrow was a day off added a warm glow inside my chest.

If only every day were a day off, I thought—but realistically, I'd be anxious if that were true. I laughed to myself.

Adachi came out holding a small can.

'Oh? You drink?' I asked.

'Sometimes.'

She looked a bit excited as we neared the park. It felt nice, walking together like this.

For the record, I can't drink at all. I tried once and made an absolute fool of myself—a human fountain. I inherited that from my mother. Unfortunately.

We reached the park between the apartment and the tall residential building. It wasn't too late, but of course no one was around. The little mound someone had built in the sandbox was still sitting there.

'There are so many flowers here.'

The bench was surrounded by plants. At the edge of the shrubs, all kinds of flowers were blooming—aside from pansies, I didn't recognize any.

'Totally unrelated but… doesn't 'pansy' sound like 'jeans' said differently?' I joked.

'…Ah. Um. Y-yes. I guess.' I received the most awkward praise imaginable.

Laughing, we sat down together. My employee ID swung lightly—I'd forgotten I still had it on. Whatever. Let it stay there. It made me feel like a real adult.

We set down our bags and leaned our shoulders together.

'Good work today,' she said.

'You too.'

We clinked her little can and my plastic bottle together and took a sip. It felt like the night gathering around us was also sinking gently into my throat.

'Want some?' I offered my juice.

'Thanks,' Adachi said with a cool smile. She used to get so shy at times like this—watching her grow while staying so close to her has been something special.

'My drink… maybe I shouldn't give you any,' she added, pulling the can back with a wry smile.

Yeah, true. If I drink, the only one who'd suffer was her. We never should've celebrated turning twenty like that…

Surrounded by flowers, we shared a quiet moment—not boring at all, not like the lethargic silence we used to have in the gym. My back felt relaxed in a way it never did at school. After a day at work, my body drank up the sweet-tart juice like dry soil.

The moon hung faintly behind thin drifting clouds. Small park lights flicked on, forming a path leading toward the apartment building like tiny lanterns guiding us home.

The flowers near the bench glowed softly under the light, making their scent feel almost stronger.

'A quiet park at night with a little alcohol… it feels like a scenic spot.'

'Hm?' Adachi tilted her head, can in hand.

'Every place feels scenic when I'm with you.'

'You too, Shimamura… uh, orange juice really suits you.'

'I'll take that as a compliment.'

What does that even mean, 'orange juice suits me'? Does she mean I go well with something sweet? Maybe it's her way of saying I'm… a lovely woman. Probably.

'Is it good?' I asked, like a child pointing at the drink.

Adachi chuckled. 'It's… okay.'

'But it makes your mood soften a little.'

'Soft and fluffy,' I repeated.

Like soda bubbles. I don't even like soda. My weaknesses just keep stacking up. At company drinking parties, I'm that person who only drinks oolong tea and eats. Dealing with drunk coworkers is exhausting… I just hold my breath and focus on the food.

Talking with people tends to be tiring, like taking unnecessary detours.

But taking detours with Adachi never feels tiring. Like tonight—I watched her profile.

…She's beautiful. Really beautiful.

As she's grown older, that transparent aura she once had has blended with her calm presence. When she's composed, she feels almost sacred in her serenity.

Though… she flails like she's drowning only when she's with me. Which would mean it's my fault… Is it my fault?

'Adachi, do people at your company ask you out to eat?' I asked, suddenly aware of how lucky I was to have this beautiful woman all to myself.

'Hmm… sometimes?'

'Ah, thought so.'

She never talked about coworkers, so I never really thought about it. But with her looks, of course people would approach her.

'Maybe sometimes,' she said again.

'I don't remember well.'

She gave an awkward little smile. If she isn't interested in someone, her attention disappears completely. Her indifference is nothing like mine—she doesn't try to soften it. If she doesn't care, she simply doesn't.

'Well, with how popular you must be, it's no surprise,' I said.

She didn't panic, or react dramatically. Yeah—nothing unexpected there.

'Really? I don't think I'm friendly,' she said.

'That's beyond the point.'

I'm not worried she'll cheat, but imagining someone pursuing her… It made me feel something I'd never felt before. Back then, we were basically always together. Now that we've had time apart, I've realized these feelings exist too.

And honestly, it's all Adachi's fault for being so cute. That's right. It's completely her fault.

'You better not cheat,' I joked lightly.

'You either. …You won't, right?'

Her eyes looked at me—not matching her relaxed smile. Her gaze was half-joking, half-serious.

'Do you think I would?'

'No, of course not.'

But her eyes didn't soften. Her standard for 'cheating' is definitely strict. If I talk to someone else for just a bit too long, she'd probably judge me guilty. If you put it that way… I must look like a serial flirt. It's impossible to live a flawless life, I guess.

'If someone else liked me… you wouldn't mind, right? Because I'm only devoted to you, Adachi-chan,' I leaned toward her, and Adachi leaned toward me in turn, until we were supporting each other's weight.

'If someone looked at you like that… I'd hate it.'

'Mmm… I figured you'd say that.'

Of course Adachi would.

'Hey, Adachi…'

I used the same tone as when I used to talk to her in the gym.

'If you ever became super rich, I could quit work and just stay home forever.'

Then I'd basically be her cat. I often think that on rough days at work.

But Adachi unexpectedly rejected the idea.

'I wouldn't… force you to stay home all day.'

'Nope. You totally would.'

'No, no, I wouldn't, not at all—'

She shook her head so hard it swayed side to side. Once again, I was reminded she had no awareness of how she really was. So she really doesn't think she's possessive, huh? If that's true… I wonder where this conversation is going to go.

'Well, what if I said I was going to hang out with my coworkers every night from now on?'

'Eh?'

'Sorry, I wouldn't actually do that.'

She sounded genuinely shocked with that 'Eh?', so I apologized immediately.

Laughing it off, I glanced at the flowers.

And just like that, we went back to being a lovey-dovey couple.

Adachi had grown calmer lately, though she still sometimes showed that… unpredictable, passionate side. Somehow, it made me feel at ease. She hadn't really changed at all. I found myself remembering who she had always been. Between what changes and what stays the same, it's the unchanging parts that bring the most comfort.

No wonder the past keeps tugging at us—because the past never changes. But Adachi… her head is filled with nothing but me… Though I'm the same.

If we were always together, we'd never get anything done, so we work at different companies—but sometimes I think it might be easier if we worked at the same place. But job hunting again would be a pain. Life isn't that simple.

Besides, being apart gives us its own flavors, its own emotions so it wasn't all bad.

I drank more orange juice—gulp, gulp. My throat burned a little from the lingering sweetness.

'I don't know why, but flowers smell stronger at night,' I said.

I leaned forward a bit, bracing my hands on the bench. Their mixed fragrances reached my nose—maybe because my vision was fading into the dark, my sense of smell felt sharper.

'I don't dislike the smell of flowers.'

They weren't as gentle as tree scents, but their bold fragrance was exciting in its own way. Leaning closer, I remembered the bouquet Adachi once gave me. Why did she give me flowers back then?

Her thoughts always stray far from my imagination. There's something lovely in that.

'Should we keep flowers in our room?' she suggested.

That sounded nice.

'You'll take care of them, right, Adachi-chan?'

'We'll do it together.'

'Okay.'

We laughed together. Lately we laugh together so often—and I think that's a good sign.

I turned my head and saw the dim streetlights lining the road, looking almost like a festival.

Since moving here, we haven't gone to a summer festival. Just sitting on a swing and catching a glimpse of distant fireworks was enough for me. Even now, these lantern-like lights were enough to make me happy.

'Adachi, are you drunk?'

'This much won't…' She paused, '…I'm a little drunk.'

She grinned mischievously—and it made me feel a bit drunk too.

'Then say something embarrassing while you're tipsy.'

'Eh—'

She immediately regretted saying that. Come on, come on, I held up my bottle like a microphone. Adachi curled her shoulders, as if the water around her were steadily rising.

Her eyes darted around, her ears turned red. She really did look drunk.

'Shimamura… your legs are really pretty.' She glanced down at my legs and then quickly looked away.

'Is that sexual harassment?'

'I-it's a compliment…'

My legs… I looked down at them, crossed neatly. If she was sneaking glances like that, no wonder I felt shy too. The night breeze wasn't strong enough to cool flushed skin—just a gentle, lingering warmth.

I wanted to compliment her legs back, but unlike mine, hers were covered. No doubt they were beautiful, but I didn't get the chance to say so.

'It's a pleasant night,' I said.

'Y-yeah.'

So I gave up.

Our conversation felt grown-up, yet threaded with the small, ordinary moments of life. It caught me off guard—how much we'd really grown. Yes—we had grown, and yet we had stayed together. And we would stay together in the days to come. Living to be with each other, and being with each other to live.

In the night air, fragrant with flowers, we shared that soft, tender feeling Adachi once mentioned.

'I wish this could last forever,' I said.

'Mm,' she replied.

I remembered—we'd had a conversation like this once before.

Thinking of the Adachi who quietly accepted my proposal, I couldn't help but smile from the bottom of my heart.
